coredata.dc:title: Realizing efficient exciton dissociation in an all-organic heterojunction photocatalyst for highly improved photocatalytic H2 evolution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Weifan
Wu, Guangyu
Liu, Qi
Zhang, Yichi
Chen, Yuwen
Han, Jiangang
Xing, Weinan
coredata.dc:description: Although graphitic carbon nitride is a promising photocatalyst in the field of energy conversion and environmental purification, the intrinsic properties like excitonic effects and sluggish charge transfer restrict further photocatalytic applications. To circumvent these limitations, the novel all-organic heterojunction photocatalysts were constructed by anchoring organic carbon dots (O-dots) on porous graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (O-dots/CNS). Results demonstrated that excitons can be eﬀectively dissociated into electrons and holes at the interface of O-dots/CNS heterojunction, followed by holes injected to O-dots and electrons accumulated in CNS to realize efficient charge separation. Consequently, the O-dots/CNS with the optimized hydrogen (H2) evolution performance could be reached 1564.5 μmol h−1g−1 under the visible light irradiation. This work not only presents new ideas for rational design photocatalytic reaction system from exciton and charge carrier, but also broaden the applications of this new kind of organic dots in the field of energy conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
Exciton dissociation
Charge separation
Photocatalytic
H2 evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure analysis of O-dots/CNS heterojunction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of O-dots/CNS heterojunction.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of different samples.
XPS spectra of (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) S 2p of different samples.
The TEM images of O-dots (a), CNS (b) and O-dots/CNS -10 (c).
(a) The H2 evolution rate of different samples and (b) stability of O-dots/CNS-10 over four consecutive runs.
(a) UV–vis DRS and (b) Band gap energy plots of different samples, (c) VB XPS spectra and (d) band structure diagrams of CNS and O-dots/CNS-10.
(a) PL spectra, (b) fluorescent lifetime, (c) transient photocurrent response and (d) EIS Nyquist plots of the CNS and O-dots/CNS-10.
Schematic illustration of the proposed photocatalytic mechanism of O-dots/CNS heterojunction under visible light illumination.

head.ce:title: Realizing efficient exciton dissociation in an all-organic heterojunction photocatalyst for highly improved photocatalytic H2 evolution

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biology and the Environment
Co-Innovation Center for the Sustainable Forestry in Southern China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although graphitic carbon nitride is a promising photocatalyst in the field of energy conversion and environmental purification, the intrinsic properties like excitonic effects and sluggish charge transfer restrict further photocatalytic applications. To circumvent these limitations, the novel all-organic heterojunction photocatalysts were constructed by anchoring organic carbon dots (O-dots) on porous graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (O-dots/CNS). Results demonstrated that excitons can be eﬀectively dissociated into electrons and holes at the interface of O-dots/CNS heterojunction, followed by holes injected to O-dots and electrons accumulated in CNS to realize efficient charge separation. Consequently, the O-dots/CNS with the optimized hydrogen (H2) evolution performance could be reached 1564.5 μmol h−1g−1 under the visible light irradiation. This work not only presents new ideas for rational design photocatalytic reaction system from exciton and charge carrier, but also broaden the applications of this new kind of organic dots in the field of energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:para: Sparked by the growing of urbanization and industrialization, energy shortages and environmental pollution have become major problems that plague mankind development [1–3]. Photocatalysis is the most promising technology to convert clean solar energy into chemical energy [4–8]. As an earth-abundant and intrinsic visible-light response photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (CN) has attracted enormous attentions [9–11]. The appropriate band gap, high stability and low cost endue CN with extensive applications in the field of photocatalysis [12,13]. However, the bulk CN only displays moderate photocatalytic activity due to the incomplete light utilization, low surface area and high recombination rate of charge carrier [14,15]. Especially the excitons of CN are more likely to recombine because of the strong Coulomb interaction, resulting in slow exciton dissociation [16,17]. Therefore, promoting the dissociation of excitons into electrons and holes could decrease the recombination efficiency of charge carriers and increase the quantity of hot electrons, thus improving the activity of the photocatalyst [18,19].

Considering the excitons are inclined to dissociate into free charges in the disordered energy landscape area [20,21]. Xiang et al. designed nitrogen vacancies modified CN ultra-thin nanosheets photocatalyst [22], with the rich nitrogen vacancies facilitated the dissociation of excitons into electrons and holes, and improved the electron density, leading to superior activity for CO2 photoreduction. Zhang et al. constructed a series of heptazine-based K+-implanted CCNs (KCCNs), the built-in electric field promoted the excitons dissociation and carrier separation, thus the optimized KCCN had increased 34 times for H2 evolution than that of pristine counterpart [23]. Inspired by these findings, constructing heterojunction is expected to optimize the exciton dissociation. Compared with individual semiconductor materials, the heterojunction often builds between two different materials [24–26], which is an optimization strategy for utilizing electric field and establishing disordered energy region, boosting exciton dissociation.

Carbon materials (for example, graphene, carbon nanotubes, and activated carbon et al.) have been broadly applied with CN to elevate the photocatalytic activity due to the special structures and better conductivity [27–29]. Different from bulk or 2D carbon materials, carbon dots have captivating advantages in the field of photocatalysis, such as the shorter carrier transport distance for better carrier separation, and the larger specific surface with abundant reactive sites [30–32]. Recently, Huang and co-workers have reported that anchoring carbon nanodots on the surface of tubular CN can greatly improve the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity owing to the synergistic effect of structural topology and heterojunction [33]. Si et al. reported [34] that the heterojunctions material of carbon quantum dots and CN could efficiently facilitate the light absorption as well as improve the charge separation and transport, thus it exhibited 14-fold increased for tetracycline degradation with CN under the visible light. Compared with carbon dots, organic carbon dots (O-dots) are prepared from organic precursors. They are easily to be functionalized via different synthesis processes and bring more attractive physical, chemical, and electronic properties. In electrochemical processes, O-dots have been frequently reported act as an excellent interface and increase the capacitance and rate performance [35]. However, few reports have been made about O-dots applications in photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Herein, we designed an all-organic heterojunction featuring with porous ultrathin sulfur-doped CN nanosheets (CNS) decorated with O-dots for efficient exciton dissociation and realizing improvements in photocatalytic H2 activity. The innovative nitrogen, sulfur doped O-dots were prepared via hydrothermal reaction with citric acid and cysteamine as precursors. The all-organic heterojunctions between O-dots and CNS boosted exciton dissociation and charge migration from O-dots to CNS. These interesting processes led to high H2 evolution performance of 1564.5 μmol h−1g−1, which is 4.2 times higher than CNS. At last, the rational photocatalytic mechanism of the O-dots/CNS photocatalyst was proposed in detail.

Cysteamine hydrochloride (C2H7NS·HCl, 98%) was obtained from Rhawn reagent company. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Trihicyanuric acid (C3H3N3S3, 95%) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical CO., Ltd.

Typically, trithiocyanuric acid was heated in a muffle furnace and kept the temperature at 520 °C for 2h with 2 °C min−1. Until cooled to room temperature, the yellow powder was obtained and marked as CNS.

The O-dots was prepared according to the previous literature with modifications [36]. A certain amount of citric acid monohydrate and cysteamine hydrochloride (mass ratio = 2:1) were added in 5.0 ml of deionized-water. After fully stirring and dissolving, the solution was heated in 80 °C for 12 h, then transferred to the Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor and heated at 200 °C for 3.5 h. The product was washed three times with deionized water after cooling to room temperature. The final product was dried at 80 °C and denoted as O-dots.

O-dots/CNS heterojunction was obtained by mixing O-dots and CNS in methanol solution. 2.5 mg, 5 mg and 10 mg of O-dots and 50 mg of CNS were mixed in 50 ml methanol by ultrasonic dispersion 30 min to obtain a homogeneous solution, respectively. Then, the magnetic stirring was used until the methanol solution volatilized completely. At last, the mixtures were dried in a constant temperature oven at 60 °C. Different mass ratios of O-dots were designated as O-dots/CNS-5、O-dots/CNS-10、O-dots/CNS-20, respectively.
The other experimental parts were added in the supporting information.

The formation processes of O-dots/CNS heterojunctions was displayed in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the O-dots were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and cysteamine as the raw material. During high temperature and pressure reaction environment, the citric acid and cysteamine are easily to lose H2O molecules to form the condensation product of organic dots embedded with nitrogen and sulfur. Then, the porous urchin CNS nanosheets could be obtained by direct thermal polycondensation of trithiocyanic acid. Finally, the O-dots are decorated on surface of CNS by electrostatic self-assembly methods, and forming the O-dots/CNS all-organic heterojunction photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure analysis of O-dots/CNS heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structures of CNS and O-dots/CNS heterojunction photocatalysts were detected by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks of pure CNS can be found at 13.3° and 27.1°, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of CNS. Diffraction peak at 13.3° is the result of repeated triazine units in plane, whereas the peak at 27.1° is attributed to the aromatic rings accumulating between layers [37]. For O-dots/CNS heterojunction, the only well-defined diffraction peaks of CNS are observed while no diffraction peaks of O-dots are exhibited for O-dots/CNS-5, which could be due to its trace amount of O-dots. With the increasing amount of O-dots, the diffraction peaks of O-dots are appeared and intensify gradually, which is consistent with the diffraction peaks in Fig. S1a [36]. Both CNS and O-dots could be observed clearly, indicating that the original structures of CNS and O-dots are maintained well, and without any impure crystalline phases. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out to analyze the chemical structure of O-dots/CNS heterojunction. The characteristic peak at about 808 cm−1 corresponds to the triazine ring unit [38], while the peaks between 1200 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 are ascribed from the aromatic CN heterocycles [39]. As well, the broad peaks between 3000 cm−1 and 3700 cm−1 belong to C–OH and C–NH [40,41]. Notably, no peaks from O-dots (Fig. S1b) are appeared from the O-dots/CNS heterojunction photocatalysts, which may be covered by the CNS. In addition, the absorption intensity of CN heterocycles is substantially reduced in O-dots/CNS, suggesting the incorporated of O-dots on CNS [30]. The similar FT-IR information of CNS and O-dots/CNS heterojunction illustrates that the chemical structure of CNS is maintained after anchoring O-dots.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on the as-prepared CNS, O-dots and O-dots/CNS-10 to determine the surface states and elements. As depicted in Fig. S2 and Fig. S3, O-dots, CNS and CNS-10 contain C, N, O, and S elements, as confirmed by the survey spectra. The XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 3 a) exhibit two peaks at 284.6 eV and 288.1 eV, which could be corresponded to CC and N–CN, respectively [42]. The N 1s spectra of CNS and O-dots/CNS-10 in Fig. 3b could be subdivided into four peaks, including the CN–C at 398.5 eV, N-(C)3 at 400.0 eV, C–N–H at 401.2 eV and the positive charge localization in heterocycles at 404.2 eV [43]. It is noteworthy that, there are slight shift of N 1s and C 1s in comparison with CNS, which further indicate the strong interaction between O-dots and CNS, as well as the electron transfer from O-dots to CNS after their coupling [18,44]. For O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), the O element contents increase from 1.8% to 3.1% for CNS and O-dots/CNS-10. The increment contents in O 1s would be induced by the O-dots in O-dots/CNS heterojunction. For pure CNS, the O 1s cored at 532.2 eV is from the surface adsorbed water [45]. A new peak at 531.1 eV is detected in the O-dots/CNS-10, which is associated with the C = O from the surface hydroxylation of O-dots [46,47]. The presence of O-dots in O-dots/CNS-10 is further reflected from the high-resolution XPS S 2p spectrum in Fig. 3d. The characteristic peak in CNS at 163.7 eV is arise from the S-doping during calcination, which replacing N atoms in some heptazine rings to form the C–S–C bonds [48]. Particularly, this peak in O-dots/CNS-10 is much stronger than that of the pure CNS, indicating the existence of O-dots in the heterojunction.

Furthermore, the microstructure and morphology of O-dots/CNS heterojunction were studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the average diameters of O-dots are less than 10 nm. The smaller size of O-dots is beneficial to short carrier transport distance of charge carriers. CNS possesses a thick 2D nanosheet-like structure with abundant mesopores (Fig. 4 b). After the addition of O-dots, some monodispersed dark dots are homogeneously deposited and closely attached on the surface of CNS. (Fig. 4c), suggesting the good interface interactions between CNS and O-dots. The above results prove that the O-dots has been successfully compounded with CNS and anchored on the surface of CNS.

Photocatalytic H2 evolution performance was carried out to explore the role of anchored O-dots on the surface of CNS. Fig. 5 a displays the photocatalytic activity of samples with different loading ratios of O-dots. The H2 evolution rate of CNS is only 372 μmol h−1 g−1 due to the strong excitonic effects and sluggish charge transfer restrict. The H2 evolution activity increases distinctly with anchored O-dots on the surface of CNS. Among all samples, the optimal H2 evolution rate for O-dots/CNS-10 could be reached to 1564.5 μmol h−1g−1, and nearly 4.2 times higher than CNS, which is higher than other CN-based photocatalysts reported previously (Table S1). The improvement of the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate could be attributed to the anchoring O-dots on the surface of CNS facilitate dissociation of excitons and charge carrier migration. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate is decreased with continued to increase the loading amounts of O-dots. The reason may be that the reactive sites on the surface of CNS are occupied by O-dots and light excitation of CNS is limited by the excess amount of O-dots. To evaluate the industrialization prospect of photocatalyst, it is important to consider the stability in addition to the photocatalytic H2 evolution rate. The O-dots/CNS-10 with the best photocatalytic performance was used as the representative for multiple cycles of photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction. In Fig. 5b, the amount of H2 evolution could be reached 6258 μmol g−1 in 4 h over O-dots/CNS-10 and almost no obvious change after four cycles during the photocatalytic process, confirming the excellent stability of O-dots/CNS-10 photocatalyst. In addition, the similar crystal structure and functional group information (Fig. S4) of O-dots/CNS-10 before and after the photocatalytic reaction further demonstrates the stability and reusability.

In order to obtain the insight of electronic properties and exciton separation over O-dots/CNS, the optical, photoelectrochemical, and luminescent decay properties were investigated in details. As the UV–visible diffuse reﬂectance spectroscopy (DRS) results in Fig. 6 , all the samples exhibit the obviously visible harvest ability. CNS displays a familiar spectrum with the light absorption edge at around 460 nm [49]. Compared with pure CNS, the absorption edges of O-dots/CNS show obvious redshift, indicating that anchoring O-dots on the surface of CNS facilitate the light absorption of O-dots/CNS. According to the Tauc plot (Fig. 6b), the corresponding band gap of BCN is calculated as 2.82 eV, which is reduced to 2.80 eV, 2.75 eV, 2.72 eV for O-dots/CNS-5, O-dots/CNS-10, O-dots/CNS-20, respectively. The narrowed band gap is attributed to the strong interactions between the O-dots and CNS, thus affecting the energy band structure. This can be further testified by the XPS valence band (VB) spectra in Fig. 6c. The VB values are determined to be 1.82 eV and 1.66 eV for CNS and O-dots/CNS-10. The schematic of band structure is showed in Fig. 6d. Evidently, the CB of O-dots/CNS-10 is more negative, suggesting the strong reduction capability of electrons for better photocatalytic H2 evolution [50,51].

The interactions between O-dots and CNS would strongly affect the excitonic process, which is elucidated by the photoluminescence spectrum (PL). As presented in Fig. 7 a, CNS exhibits an evident emission peak due to the recombination of self-trapped excitons [52]. After the introduction of O-dots, PL intensity dramatically dropped in O-dot/CNS-10. The weaker PL peak indicates that the number of singlet excitons is decreased [53,54]. That is to say, the O-dots/CNS-10 heterojunction can profit from the presence of O-dots and the strong interaction between interfaces, promoting the excitons dissociation and restraining the recombination of charge carriers. The lifetime of singlet excitons was determined by the corresponding time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy (TRPL) in Fig. 7b. The TRPL spectra of O-dots/CNS-10 heterojunction shows a downward trend from the pure CNS, which further demonstrates that the as-prepared O-dots/CNS-10 can effectively promote the exciton dissociation [55,56].

Through photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), the interfacial charge separation, migration, and transfer behaviors have been explored. Fig. 7c illustrates the photocurrent response of CNS and O-dots/CNS-10 under the light illumination. The photocurrent response intensity of O-dots/CNS-10 increases significantly relative to CNS and remains stable on each on-off cycles. The enhanced photocurrent density of O-dots/CNS-10 validates the higher the charge separation efficiency after anchoring O-dots on the surface of CNS [57]. Apart from that EIS was used to further study the charge transfer process (Fig. 7d). Generally, charge transfer resistance tends to be lower when the Nyquist plot radius is smaller [58]. Compared with CNS, the EIS arc of O-dots/CNS-10 has a distinct smaller radius, implying a higher interfacial charge transfer rate. To sum up, the formation of heterojunction between O-dots and CNS significantly promotes exciton dissociation and charge carrier separation.

Combining the above experimental and characterization results, a complete understanding of the exciton dissociation processes in O-dots/CNS is described in Fig. 8 . When the O-dots/CNS heterojunction catalyst is irradiated by the visible light, large amounts of electron-hole pairs are excited from CNS [59]. Coulomb interactions increase their likelihood of bonding together to form excitons. However, with the aid of the difference of heterojunction interface between O-dots and CNS, excitons in CNS could be dissociated into free electrons and holes. As well, the O-dots with negatively charged edge functional groups assist in attracting and accelerating the transfer of holes in O-dots that were excited. Namely, the holes tend to inject from CNS to O-dots, and the electrons accumulate in CNS, which significantly facilitating excitons dissociate and suppressing the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole. Furthermore, the ultrathin porous nanostructure provides more active sites for the reaction and speeding up electron transfer. Benefiting from the unique photoexcitation process for H2 evolution, a rapid electron transfer lead to the reduction of protons to form H2. At the same time, the hole with oxidation ability is consumed by TEOA.

In summary, the novel all-organic O-dots/CNS heterojunction photocatalysts have been developed via a facile self-assembly method. The obtained heterojunction photocatalyst could efficiently splitting water with the optimized H2 evolution rate up to 1564.5 μmol h−1 g−1, which is higher than other CN-based photocatalysts reported previously. By the joint observations from the characterization result, it testified that the inducing of O-dots on the surface of CNS could adjust the dissociation of excitons and facilitate charge carrier migration. Compared with CNS, the CB of O-dots/CNS10 has a negative shift, indicating the strong reduction capability of electrons for high efficiency H2 evolution. This work focused on excitonic effects of CN photocatalyst, and constructed metal-free heterojunction interface to realize the targeted regulation of the behavior of excitons, further enhancing the efficiency of charge carriers utilization during photocatalytic reactions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (21901119), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (2019M661850), Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research Funds (2021K022A, 2020Z098), The Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_1100), and the Analysis and Test Center of Nanjing Forestry University.
